1: Isaiah 44:1 opens the reassurance oracle after Isaiah 43's indictment and grace: 've'attah shema Ya'aqov avdi, but now hear, Jacob my servant.' The 've'attah (but now) = the same pivot as 43:1 and Isaiah 44:21, marking the transition from accusation back to comfort. 'Israel, whom I have chosen' (Yisrael asher bechharti = Israel whom I chose) — the divine election standing despite the sins catalogued in Isaiah 43:22-28. The 'hearing' command (shema) frames the oracle as something Israel must actively attend to, not passively receive — the covenant summons to alertness.
2: 'This is what the LORD says — he who made you, who formed you in the womb, and who will help you: Do not be afraid, Jacob my servant, Jeshurun whom I have chosen' — the Jeshurun address: Yeshurun (from yashar = upright, possibly a diminutive of affection, the idealized Israel) appears only four times in the OT (Deuteronomy 32:15; Isaiah 33:5, 26; Isaiah 44:2) — always as a poetic name for Israel at its covenant best, as YHWH envisions it. 'Formed you in the womb' (yotsrekha mibbeten = the one who formed you from the womb) grounds divine faithfulness in Israel's very creation. The same 'formed in the womb' applies to Jeremiah's prophetic call (Jeremiah 1:5) and to the Servant (Isaiah 49:5) — YHWH's purposes are prenatal, not contingent on subsequent behavior.
3: 'For I will pour water on the thirsty land and streams on the dry ground; I will pour out my Spirit on your offspring, and my blessing on your children' — the double pouring: (1) 'ettsog mayim al-tsame = I will pour water on the thirsty land' and 'nozlim al-yabbashah = streams on the dry ground' = the physical water-miracle of the new Exodus (Isaiah 41:17-18; Isaiah 43:19-20); (2) 'ettsog ruchi al-zar'ekha = I will pour out my Spirit on your offspring.' The ruach (Spirit = the same Spirit that hovered over the waters in Genesis 1:2; that rested on the 70 elders in Numbers 11:25-29; that YHWH promised through Ezekiel 36:27: 'I will put my Spirit in you') poured out on the descendants. Joel 2:28 expands this into the great Spirit-promise: 'I will pour out my Spirit on all flesh' — quoted at Pentecost (Acts 2:17-18). The physical water and the spiritual outpouring are parallel: both are divine gifts that produce new life in the wasteland.
4: 'They will spring up among the grass like willows beside flowing streams' — the botanical flourishing of the Spirit-watered community: 'vetsamchu bein chatsir ke'aravim al-yivlei mayim, they will spring up among the grass like willows beside flowing streams.' The chatsir (grass) and aravim (willows/poplars = the moisture-dependent trees of Psalms 137:2: 'we hung our harps on the willows there') = the two vegetation types of a well-watered environment. The community that receives the Spirit-pouring is like the lush vegetation beside a stream: rooted, drinking continuously, growing naturally. The contrast with the idolaters who are 'feeding on ashes' (Isaiah 44:20) and the wilderness that will be transformed (Isaiah 41:18-19).
5: 'One will say, I belong to the LORD, and another will take the name Jacob as his own. One will write on his hand, The LORD's, and will be called by the name of Israel' — the voluntary gentile identification with Israel's covenant community: four acts of covenant-confession by previously unaffiliated people: (1) verbal declaration ('I belong to YHWH' = ani leLYHWH = the possessive formula of covenant belonging); (2) adopting the name Jacob (yiqra beshem-Ya'aqov = will call himself by the name Jacob); (3) inscribing on the hand 'The LORD's' (yiktov yado leLYHWH = will write on his hand: to YHWH) — the tattooing or writing of divine ownership on the body, inverting the slave-mark and anticipating Revelation 7:3's seal of God on the servants' foreheads; (4) taking the name Israel. These are descriptions of gentiles voluntarily and completely identifying with YHWH's covenant people — one of the clearest OT anticipations of the universal church.
6: 'This is what the LORD says — Israel's King and Redeemer, the LORD of hosts: I am the first and I am the last; apart from me there is no God' — the most compressed form of the incomparability declaration: 'ani rishon va'ani acharon umibbal'adai en Elohim = I am the first and I am the last, and apart from me there is no God.' The three designations: (1) Israel's King (melek Yisrael = the divine kingship over the covenant community); (2) Israel's Redeemer (go'alo = his kinsman-redeemer, the one who pays the redemption price); (3) YHWH of hosts (the divine warrior). Together they establish YHWH's credentials for the claim. 'I am the first and last' (rishon ve'acharon) = the temporal frame: YHWH predates all and will outlast all. This formula appears in Revelation 1:17; Isaiah 2:8; 22:13 applied to the risen Christ — the Christological identification is explicit in John's Apocalypse.
7: 'Who is like me? Let him come forward, announce it, and lay it out before me. Who has declared from of old what is to come, since I established the ancient people? Let them tell us what lies ahead' — the incomparability challenge with the prediction-test: 'umi khemoni yiqra veyaggideha ve'ya'arkhehah li = who is like me, let him call out and declare it and set it before me.' The court-of-evidence standard: any divine claimant must be able to announce in advance what is coming. 'Since I established the ancient people' (misiml'i am-olam = since my placing of the people of eternity) = since the founding of Israel as YHWH's covenant people from primordial time. The challenge is specifically about the Cyrus-oracle: no other deity or divination system declared Cyrus's role in advance.
8: 'Do not tremble, do not be afraid. Did I not tell you long ago and declare it? You are my witnesses: is there any God apart from me? There is no Rock — I know of none' — the Rock-claim: 'hatsur = the Rock' (from tsur = the craggy rock of the desert, the immovable stronghold; Deuteronomy 32:4: 'the Rock, his work is perfect, for all his ways are justice'; Psalms 18:2: 'YHWH is my rock, my fortress, my deliverer; my God is my rock, in whom I take refuge'). 'There is no Rock — I know of none' (ve'en tsur bal-yadati = and there is no rock; not one I know of) = the emphatic denial: not just that other divine rocks are weaker than YHWH, but that YHWH in his comprehensive knowledge finds no other rock-deity in existence. 1 Cor 10:4 identifies Christ as the Rock who followed Israel in the wilderness: the divine Rock of Isaiah becomes the pre-incarnate Christ.
9: 'All who fashion idols are worthless, and the things they prize are of no use. Their own witnesses can neither see nor know — so they will be put to shame' — the verdict on idol-makers: 'yotserei fessel kullam tohu = fashioners of idol, all of them are chaos/nothing.' The tohu (chaos = the same pre-creation void of Genesis 1:2 applied in 41:29 to the idols' works) applied to the idol-makers themselves — the craftsmen share the ontological status of their creation. 'The things they prize are of no use' (vachamodeihem bal-yo'ilu = and their precious things are of no profit) — the emotional investment in idols ('prize/cherish them') does not compensate for their functional uselessness. 'Their own witnesses can neither see nor know' (vedeihem hemma bal-yiru vebal-yede'u = their witnesses they are, they cannot see and they cannot know) — the idols' own 'witnesses' (the worshipers who testify to their effectiveness) are themselves blind and ignorant.
10: 'Who would shape a god or cast a metal idol that profits him nothing?' — the rhetorical absurdity: 'mi-yatsar el = who shapes a god?' The question expects the answer: no rational person would. 'Cast a metal idol that profits him nothing' (umassekha yatsaq lelo yo'il = and a molten image pour out — for no profit) — the yo'il (profit/benefit = from ya'al = to be of benefit, the pragmatic test: does it help?) applied to the fundamental value-proposition of idol-worship. The entire idol-polemic of vv9-20 is essentially a sustained pragmatic argument: the idol costs enormous labor to produce and returns zero benefit.
11: 'Look — all his associates will be put to shame, and the craftsmen are, after all, only human. Let them assemble together and stand up; they will be terrified and shamed together' — the guild of shame: 'hen kol-chaveiro yevoshu = see, all his associates/fellows will be shamed.' The chaverim (associates/members of the same guild = the craftsmen's professional guild who collaborate in idol-production) share the shame of their product. 'The craftsmen are, after all, only human' (vecharashim hemmah me'adam = and the craftsmen — they are from human/of humanity) — the point: the makers of 'gods' are themselves merely human beings. 'Let them assemble together and stand up' (yitqabetsu kulam ya'amodu = let them all assemble and stand) — the challenge to the assembled idol-guild: stand and defend your craft. 'They will be terrified and shamed together' (yifchadu veyevoshu yachdav = they will fear and be shamed together).
12: 'The blacksmith works the iron over burning coals; he beats it into shape with hammers, forging it with his strong arm. But he grows hungry and loses his strength; if he drinks no water, he grows faint' — the iron-idol's maker is himself vulnerable: 'charash barzel ma'atsad vepa'al bapecham = the iron-craftsman with a cutting tool works in the coals.' The process: charash barzel (blacksmith) works in the coals (bapecham = in the burning coal-fire), beats into shape (vayyitseruhu bepattishim = hammers it into shape), and forges (vyep'alehu bizroa kocho = works it with his arm of strength). The irony: 'but he grows hungry and loses his strength' (gam-ra'ev ve'en koach = and also he is hungry and has no strength). 'If he drinks no water, he grows faint' (lo-shata mayim vayyi'af = has not drunk water and grows faint). The one making the 'almighty god' cannot sustain himself through the manufacturing process — he needs water breaks. The creator of the alleged deity is dependent on the same physiological necessities as any laborer.
13: 'The carpenter measures the wood with a line; he draws an outline with chalk. He shapes it with chisels and marks it with a compass, carving it into the shape of a man — a handsome human figure — to be set up in a house' — the detailed woodworking process: 'charash etsim natah qav = the wood-craftsman stretches a measuring line.' The surveyor's tools applied to idol-making: (1) qav (measuring line = the carpenter's chalk-line for marking straight cuts); (2) maqtsuah (a chalk/marker = the marking tool); (3) maatsad (a chisel = the shaping tool); (4) mechuga (a compass = the instrument for drawing circles and ellipses). The precision tooling: the idolater uses the most sophisticated carpentry tools of the ancient world to produce 'a handsome human figure' (ketavnit ish kemireh adam = like the beauty of a man, like the appearance of a human). The irony: YHWH made humans in his image (Genesis 1:26-27); the idolater makes his god in the image of a human. The sequence is inverted.
14: 'He cuts down cedars; he selects a cypress or an oak and lets it grow strong among the trees of the forest. He planted it, and the rain made it grow' — the origin of the idol-material: 'likrot lo arazim = to cut for himself cedars' (the prestigious Lebanon cedar) or 'yiqach tirzah veallon = he takes a holm-oak or an oak.' 'He planted it, and the rain made it grow' (nata oren vageshem yegaddel = he planted a pine and the rain made it great). The theological point: the wood that becomes the idol was planted by the idolater and grew by YHWH's provision of rain. The rain that made the tree grow is the same rain that YHWH provides (Job 5:10: 'he gives rain on the earth and sends water on the fields'). YHWH's sustaining of creation provides the raw material for the idol that denies him.
15: 'Then the wood serves as fuel for the man. He burns part of it to warm himself; he kindles a fire and bakes bread. But he also fashions a god from it and worships it — carves an idol and bows down to it' — the bifurcation of purpose: 'vehayah le'adam levarer = and it is for a man for fuel.' Part one: 'yiqqach mehem vayycham af-yasiq veyofeh lechem = he takes from them and warms himself; he also kindles and bakes bread.' Part two: 'af-yipsol el vayishtachu asah pessel vayisgod-lamo = he also fashions a god and worships it; he made a carved image and bows down to it.' The same tree, the same hands, the same day — fuel in the morning, deity in the afternoon. The satire exposes the arbitrariness of idolaters' category distinctions: what makes one half 'firewood' and the other half 'God' is purely the craftsman's decision, not any property of the material itself.
16: 'Half of it he burns in the fire; over the coals he roasts meat, eats his fill, and warms himself, saying, Aha, I am warm; I can feel the heat' — the half-and-half narrative: 'chatsiyo saraf bemoh-esh, half of it he burned in the fire.' The meals and warmth from half: 'al-chatsiyo basar yochal = over half of it he roasts meat'; 'yishba af-yicham ve'yomar heach chamoti, he eats his fill and also warms himself and says: aha! I am warm; I have seen the fire!' The 'aha!' (heach = the exclamation of satisfaction, the sound of a person who has gotten what they needed from the fire) is the embodiment of treating fire as pure utility — the physical pleasure of warmth and roasted meat. The idolater cannot perceive the theological absurdity because his belly is full and he is warm.
17: 'But the rest of the wood he makes into his god, into a carved image, and bows down to it and worships it. He prays to it and says, Save me — you are my god' — the other half: 'usheerito le'el asah lefesellov yisgod-lo = and the rest of it into a god he makes; his carved image he bows down to it.' 'He prays to it and says, Save me — you are my god' (yitpallel elav veyomar hatsiyleni ki eli-attah = he prays to it and says: save me, for you are my god). The prayer ('save me') directed to the piece of wood that survived the cooking fire. The same root yatsal (to save/deliver) that YHWH uses of himself throughout these chapters: 'apart from me there is no savior' (Isaiah 43:11). The wooden object that just cooked last night's meat is now the object of the most urgent human prayer: 'save me.'
18: 'They have no knowledge or understanding; their eyes are plastered shut so they cannot see, and their minds are closed so they cannot grasp it' — the anatomy of spiritual blindness: 'lo yade'u velo yavinu = they have no knowledge and no understanding.' 'Their eyes are plastered shut so they cannot see' (ki tach me'rot eineihem mire'ot = for their eyes are smeared over from seeing) — tach (to plaster/smear = the daubing of plaster over a surface to seal it; the same root as the plastering of a house in Leviticus 14:42). The eyes are sealed over, deliberately plastered shut. 'And their minds are closed so they cannot grasp it' (levotham mahahskel = their hearts from understanding). The theological diagnosis: idolaters are not in the grip of an understandable error but in a condition of willful blindness — the rational faculty itself has been sealed against the obvious evidence.
19: 'No one stops to think; no one has the knowledge or insight to say: Half of this I burned — I even baked bread over its coals; I roasted meat and ate it. Should I really make an abomination out of what is left? Should I bow down to a block of wood?' — the absence of rational self-reflection: 'velo-yashiv el-libbo velo da'at velo tevunah, and not one returns it to his heart; and there is no knowledge and no understanding.' The returning-to-one's-heart (yashuv el-libbo = the OT phrase for rational reflection, self-examination: 1 Kings 8:47; Job 1:5) is absent. The unavailable question: 'halo shethi lo aseiti gam-aseiti al-gahalav = is it not that half of it I burned? I even baked bread on its coals.' 'Should I really make an abomination out of what is left?' (avudul lish'er yisgod = and from the rest of it an abomination shall I make?) — toevah (abomination) = the strongest term of moral-religious offense. The idolater cannot ask the obvious question because his mind is sealed (v18).
20: 'He is feeding on ashes; a deceived mind has led him astray. He cannot save himself or bring himself to say, Is this thing in my hand not a fraud?' — the diagnosis: 'ro'eh efer lev huttal hittahu = he feeds on ashes; a deceived heart has turned him aside.' The efer (ashes = the final residue of what was burned — he feeds on ashes because his 'god' is a piece of wood from the same tree that produced the cooking fire's ashes). The huttal (deceived = from natal = to be misled, diverted from the path; the same root as the deception of Eve in Genesis 3:13: 'the serpent deceived me'). 'He cannot save himself' (velo yatsal et-nafshov = and he cannot deliver his soul) — the ultimate irony: the idol he prays to for salvation ('save me' v17) cannot be recognized as fraudulent because the idolater's mind cannot form the question. 'Is this thing in my hand not a fraud?' (hasheger beyemini = is the lie in my right hand?) — the self-incriminating question the sealed mind cannot ask.
21: 'Remember all this, Jacob and Israel, for you are my servant. I formed you; you are my servant. Israel, I will not forget you' — the return to Israel after the idol-polemic: 'zekhorah elleh Ya'aqov veYisrael ki avdi-attah, remember these things, Jacob and Israel, for you are my servant.' The idol-polemic (vv9-20) was implicitly addressed to the exiles who were surrounded by Babylonian idol-worship — the reminder 'you are my servant' reasserts Israel's distinct identity after the extended satire. 'I will not forget you' (lo enshakehah = I will not forget you) — the divine memory of Israel: the covenant commitment that means YHWH does not lose track of his people even in Babylon. Isaiah 49:15-16: 'Can a mother forget the baby at her breast? ...I will not forget you. See, I have engraved you on the palms of my hands.'
22: 'I have swept away your transgressions like a cloud, your sins like morning mist. Return to me, for I have redeemed you' — the forgiveness oracle: 'machiti kha'av pisha'akha = I have wiped away like a cloud your transgressions.' The machah (to wipe/blot out = the same root as Isaiah 43:25: 'I am he who blots out your transgressions') applied to a cloud (av = a dense, heavy thundercloud) and morning mist (khe'anan = the thin early morning fog that dissipates when the sun rises). The transgressions are like clouds in two senses: (1) they obscure the relationship with YHWH as clouds obscure the sun; (2) they are impermanent — wiped away as easily as a cloud by YHWH's act. 'Return to me, for I have redeemed you' (shuva elai ki ge'altikhah = return to me, for I have redeemed you) — the redemption (perfect tense: already accomplished in YHWH's purpose) as the ground for the call to return. The sequence: YHWH acts first (forgives, redeems), then calls for the human response (return/repent).
23: 'Shout for joy, O heavens, for the LORD has done it! Shout aloud, O depths of the earth! Burst into song, O mountains, O forest, every tree in it! For the LORD has redeemed Jacob and displays his glory in Israel' — the cosmic celebration: 'ronu shamayim ki-asah YHWH = sing for joy, O heavens, for YHWH has done it!' The full creation invited to celebrate: heavens (shamayim), depths of the earth (tachtiyot erets), mountains (harim), and the entire forest (ya'ar vekhol-ets bo). The non-human creation as worship-community: trees and mountains singing is the eschatological vision of creation's liberation (Romans 8:21: 'the creation itself will be liberated from its bondage to decay'). 'The LORD has redeemed Jacob' (ki ga'al YHWH Ya'aqov = for YHWH has redeemed Jacob) and 'displays his glory in Israel' (uveYisrael yitpa'ar = and in Israel he glorifies himself) — the redemption of Israel is the display of YHWH's kavod to all creation.
24: 'This is what the LORD says — your Redeemer, who formed you in the womb: I am the LORD who made all things; I alone stretched out the heavens; by myself I spread out the earth' — the Creator's credentials for the Cyrus-oracle: 'ko-amar YHWH go'alekhah veyotsrekha mibbeten = thus says YHWH your redeemer and your former from the womb.' Three creation claims: (1) 'I am the LORD who made all things' (anoki YHWH oseh kol = I, YHWH, the maker of everything); (2) 'I alone stretched out the heavens' (noteh shamayim levaddi = the one who stretched out the heavens by myself/alone); (3) 'by myself I spread out the earth' (roqa ha'arets mi-itti = the one who spread out the earth — who was with me?). The mi-itti (who was with me?) = the rhetorical question: no one. YHWH stretched out the heavens alone — no partner, no divine council member, no demiurge. This is the creation-credential that authorizes him to appoint Cyrus.
25: 'Who thwarts the omens of impostors and makes fools of diviners, who turns back the wise and makes their knowledge into nonsense' — YHWH's counter-operation against Babylon's knowledge systems: 'meper otot baddim = the one who thwarts/breaks the signs of liars/diviners.' The baddim (boasters/imposters = the false prophets and professional diviners who claimed predictive knowledge; Jeremiah 50:36: 'a sword against her false prophets! They will become fools'). 'Makes fools of diviners' (veqosemim yeholelel = he makes the diviners into madmen/fools) — the holelim (mad ones = those who are deranged; Ecclesiastes 1:17; Isaiah 2:2: 'what is done is madness'). The Babylonian astronomical and omen literature represented the most sophisticated knowledge system in the ANE; YHWH dismisses it as the work of baddim. 'Turns back the wise and makes their knowledge into nonsense' (meshib chakhamim achor veda'tam yesakkeil = the one who turns back the wise and makes their knowledge foolish) — the wisdom of Babylon (cf. 1 Corinthians 1:20: 'has not God made foolish the wisdom of the world?').
26: 'Who fulfills the word of his servant and carries out the plan of his messengers, who says about Jerusalem, It will be inhabited, and about the towns of Judah, They will be rebuilt, and I will raise up their ruins' — the contrast with Babylon's diviners: YHWH validates not the omens of impostors (v25) but 'the word of his servant' (devar avdo = the prophetic word of his servant = Isaiah's oracles). 'The plan of his messengers' (atzat mala'akhav = the counsel of his ambassadors/messengers = the prophetic messages). 'Says about Jerusalem, It will be inhabited' (ha'omer liYrushalayim tushekhab = who says to Jerusalem: she will be inhabited) — the specific fulfillment: Jerusalem rebuilt after Babylon. 'About the towns of Judah, They will be rebuilt' (velehare Yehudah tivvaneynah = and the cities of Judah they will be rebuilt). These are the oracles whose fulfillment through Cyrus validates the prophetic office and exposes the Babylonian diviners.
27: 'Who commands the ocean depths, Dry up! and says to the rivers, I will make you run dry' — the Exodus-allusion and Cyrus's campaign: 'ha'omer latsulah choravi = the one who says to the deep: dry up!' The tsulah (the deep/ocean = the same tehom of Genesis 1:2 and Exodus 15:5: 'the deep covers them') commanded to dry up. This double-references: (1) the Red Sea crossing (Exodus 14:21-22) where YHWH dried the sea; (2) Cyrus's diversion of the Euphrates when he entered Babylon (539 BCE) — Herodotus (1.189-191) describes how Cyrus diverted the Euphrates into a lake to expose the riverbed as an entry point under Babylon's walls. The same YHWH who dried the Red Sea dried the Euphrates for Cyrus.
28: 'Who says of Cyrus, He is my shepherd; he will carry out everything I want him to do, who says of Jerusalem, She will be rebuilt, and of the temple, Your foundation will be laid' — the climactic Cyrus-naming: 'ha'omer leKhoresh ro'i = the one who says to Cyrus: my shepherd.' The naming of Cyrus (Hebrew: Koresh = Persian: Kuraš = Greek: Kyros) by name approximately 150 years before his birth is the most dramatic fulfilled-prophecy claim in Isaiah. The title ro'i (my shepherd = the shepherd-king of YHWH's choosing, the same term as the messianic shepherd of Ezekiel 34:23; 37:24 applied to the Davidic king) given to a pagan Persian king. 'He will carry out everything I want him to do' (vekhol-chefetsi yashlem = and all my pleasure/desire he will complete) — Cyrus's career is the fulfillment of YHWH's divine counsel (chafets). The historical Cyrus Cylinder (538 BCE) confirms: Cyrus decreed the return of all exiled peoples to their homelands and the rebuilding of their temples — including the Jerusalem temple (Ezra 1:1-4; Isaiah 6:3-5).
